event: Portuguese navigator João da Nova discovers the uninhabited volcanic island, reputedly on the feast day of Saint Helena, giving the island its name.
Portuguese exile Fernão Lopes is marooned on the island and lives there as a hermit, becoming its first long-term resident.
English navigator Thomas Cavendish becomes the first Englishman known to visit the island during his circumnavigation of the globe.
The Dutch Republic formally claims Saint Helena, though they never permanently settle it.
The English East India Company takes possession of the island and establishes a fortified settlement at what becomes Jamestown.
The Dutch briefly seize the island before English forces recapture it, after which a royal charter confirms East India Company control.
Astronomer Edmond Halley arrives to catalogue the stars of the Southern Hemisphere, producing the first such catalogue from the island.
The East India Company bans the further importation of enslaved people to the island, an early step toward emancipation.
Napoleon Bonaparte is exiled to Saint Helena after his defeat at Waterloo, held under British guard at Longwood House.
Napoleon dies at Longwood House and is buried on the island until his remains are returned to France in 1840.
Slavery effectively ends on the island as the East India Company frees the remaining enslaved population.
Control of Saint Helena passes from the East India Company to the British Crown, making it a Crown colony.
Saint Helena becomes a base for the Royal Navy's West Africa Squadron suppressing the Atlantic slave trade, with thousands of liberated Africans landed at Rupert's Valley.
France is granted ownership of Napoleon's tomb and Longwood House, which remain French government property to this day.
The opening of the Suez Canal diverts shipping away from the island, beginning a long economic decline.
Zulu king Dinuzulu is exiled to Saint Helena by the British following his resistance in Zululand.
Around 6,000 Boer prisoners of war are interned on the island during the Second Boer War.
Ascension Island is made a dependency of Saint Helena, administered from Jamestown.
Tristan da Cunha is formally made a dependency of Saint Helena.
Flax production, the island's economic mainstay, collapses after the British Post Office switches to synthetic twine, deepening dependence on UK subsidies.
The British Nationality Act strips St Helenians of full British citizenship and the automatic right to live in the UK, prompting widespread protest.
The new Royal Mail Ship St Helena enters service, providing the island's main lifeline link to the outside world for passengers and cargo.
The British Overseas Territories Act restores full British citizenship to St Helenians, granting them the right of abode in the UK.
A new constitution comes into force, renaming the territory Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha and giving each island its own governance.
Saint Helena Airport is completed near Prosperous Bay, though commercial flights are delayed due to dangerous wind shear on the runway.
Scheduled commercial air service from Johannesburg begins, ending the island's reliance on the RMS St Helena and opening it to tourism.
The RMS St Helena is decommissioned after the airport's air link becomes the primary means of access to the island.
